results.possibleIdentity: Vicki Hodgetts is a feminist artist and writer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is best known for her pivotal role in the 'Womanhouse' exhibition in 1972, where she co-created the 'Nurturant Kitchen' installation. This work featured the 'Eggs to Breasts' piece, consisting of three-dimensional fried eggs on the ceiling that morphed into breasts on the walls, exploring themes of domesticity and nurturing. Prior to her involvement with the Feminist Art Program at the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts), she was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Specifically, she contributed to the Pepsi Pavilion project for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where she worked as a copywriter and was involved in the project's documentation and communications. Her transition from the collaborative, technology-focused environment of E.A.T. to the consciousness-raising collective of the Feminist Art Program marked a significant shift in her career toward feminist-centered installation and performance art.

results.possibleIdentity: Adrianne Wortzel (born October 7, 1941) is an American pioneering media artist, researcher, and educator known for her work at the intersection of art, robotics, and technology. Born in Brooklyn, she began her art studies at the Brooklyn Museum Art School (1956–1963) and graduated from Brooklyn College in 1963, where she studied under prominent figures such as Ad Reinhardt, Mark Rothko, Jimmy Ernst, and Louise Bourgeois. In the mid-1960s, her work included abstract paintings and geometric motorized sculptures that were precursors to her later time-based and robotic art. During the 1970s, she began exhibiting her work internationally, including solo exhibitions in Sweden and the United States. Wortzel is a pioneer in telerobotics and performance art; since the 1990s, she has integrated robotics and networked systems into her installations and theatrical productions. She is the Founding Director of StudioBlueLab and a Professor Emerita at the New York City College of Technology (CUNY). Her work has been supported by organizations that promote art-science collaboration, including the National Science Foundation and the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation, and she is frequently cited in historical overviews of the art and technology movement that began with groups like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer (likely the individual referred to as Riselly Mauer) was a Romanian-born sculptor active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is historically documented as a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Specifically, she collaborated with mechanical engineer Ben-Ami Kadish (1923–2012) on a project submitted to the 1968 E.A.T. competition. This collaboration resulted in her inclusion in the landmark exhibition "Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes," held at the Brooklyn Museum from November 1968 to January 1969. Born around 1930, Mauer's work often involved metal sculpture, including a modern metal piece she unveiled at the Hillel Center in New Jersey in 1972. Earlier in her career, she was involved in community art and children's programs in Montreal and New Jersey. Despite her participation in significant avant-garde circles, she remains one of the lesser-documented women artists associated with the E.A.T. organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Steven Robert Snyder (1945–1985), widely known by his artistic pseudonym Ralston Farina, was a New York-based performance and visual artist active from the late 1960s through the early 1980s. In 1969, he began performing at the Poetry Project under the moniker 'Steven Raven.' He was a significant figure in the downtown Manhattan art scene, particularly associated with the alternative spaces movement including The Kitchen, 112 Greene Street, and Artists Space. His work, which he termed 'Zeitkunst' (Time Art), was centered on the experience and philosophy of time, which he considered his primary medium. Snyder/Farina was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; notably, he was included in the E.A.T. 'Artcash' project in 1971. His aesthetic was influenced by peers like Laurie Anderson and Scott Burton and encouraged by John Cage. For much of his career, he forbade documentation of his work, which contributed to his relative obscurity until recent archival exhibitions. He was also featured in major international exhibitions such as the Paris Biennale and Documenta 6 in 1977.

results.possibleIdentity: Jacob Perlmutter is a contemporary British filmmaker, photographer, and musician born in the late 1980s. He is best known for directing the 2024 feature-length documentary 'Two Strangers Trying Not To Kill Each Other,' which follows the relationship of renowned street photographer Joel Meyerowitz and artist Maggie Barrett. Perlmutter began his career at age 16 as a photographer for NME and later graduated with a BA in Film Production from the Arts University Bournemouth in 2011. While he was not active in the 1960s or 1970s, his work often engages with that era's artistic legacy; for example, he shot a photo essay titled '88 Days' in 2008 as an homage to Robert Frank’s 1958 project 'The Americans.' Recent art publications, such as Aesthetica, have profiled his film alongside historical retrospectives of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which may lead to contemporary associations with that organization.

Jack Perlmutter (1920–2006) was an American painter, printmaker, and educator who was highly active during the late 1960s and 1970s. Based in Washington, D.C., he served as the Chairman of the Graphics and Printmaking Department at the Corcoran Gallery School of Art from 1960 to 1982. Perlmutter is notable for his association with major technological and scientific endeavors of the era through the NASA Art Program, which commissioned him to document space missions including the Saturn V, Apollo 6, Apollo 16, and Skylab. His work from this period, which often combined semi-abstract urban scenes with depictions of space technology, was featured in the landmark 1971 book 'Eyewitness to Space' and exhibited at the National Gallery of Art and the Smithsonian. Although typically referred to as 'Jack,' his profile matches the timeframe and the 'art and technology' thematic link requested.

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Newman is a British artist, cartoonist, and illustrator born in 1947. He was active in the London art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s, having attended the Harrow School of Art from 1959 to 1964, where his teachers included Sir Peter Blake and Ken Howard. He is known for a diverse career spanning both commercial illustration and fine art. In his fine art practice, he has produced paintings that frequently embed elements of contemporary urban life within unconventional landscapes, such as his work featuring processed peas or rotting fruit. One of his notable paintings, titled 'Performance', was acquired by the Swindon Museum and Art Gallery in 2000. His commercial work is famously associated with the design or illustration of the 'Churchill' insurance dog. Newman has been involved in projects that bridge the gap between art and technology, a theme consistent with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Furthermore, a 'Martin Newman' is recorded as an artist member in the archival matching records of E.A.T., reflecting the organization's mission to pair artists with engineers for technical collaborations.

Martin Newman is a creative director and experimental artist who has been involved in large-scale installations that integrate art and technology. He notably served as the Creative Director for the 'Empire of Silence' (L’Empire du Silence) pavilion at the Expo.02 Swiss National Exhibition in 2002. This project was a 20-minute immersive experience exploring communication and sensory deprivation through high-tech interactive screens and motion-reactive elements. Given his role in such a significant technological art project, he represents a likely candidate for the artist associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) tradition. This identity aligns with a professional who was active in the early 1970s and continued into the late 20th and early 21st centuries, focusing on the intersection of technical engineering and artistic performance.

results.possibleIdentity: Megan Bierman (born 1950) is a multi-disciplinary artist, musician, and dancer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly within experimental and collaborative performance circles. She attended Bennington College from 1968 to 1972, where she studied and performed with the Judith Dunn - Bill Dixon Dance Company, a group known for its ground-breaking improvisational and interdisciplinary work. In December 1969, she was a member of the Bill Dixon Ensemble during a performance at the Carriage Barn at Bennington College, which involved both music and movement. Her early collaborative work extended into the 1970s with projects such as "Megacycles," a collaboration with fellow Bennington graduate Laurel Sprigg, John Gruntfest, and Joe Sabella. Bierman's practice is notably multi-modal, involving the tenor saxophone (which she has played for over 40 years), improvisational dance, painting, drawing, and storytelling. In the 1980s, she was part of the musical storytelling duo The Echo Sisters with Sally Davis. She later received a Masters of Science in Education from Bank Street College in 1998 and has served as an artist-in-residence at the Buen Dia Family School in San Francisco for nearly three decades. Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is supported by her involvement in the avant-garde dance and music scenes of the late 1960s that overlapped with E.A.T.'s collaborative mission, as well as the presence of E.A.T. documentation in archival collections (such as the Remy Charlip Collection) that document the same experimental performance circles in which she was active.
